coredata.dc:title: Spectrofluorimetric determination of bupropion using N,S co-doped carbon quantum dots: Mechanistic investigation, response surface optimization, and application to pharmaceutical formulations, spiked plasma and environmental samples
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Almutairi, Farooq M.
Althobaiti, Yusuf S.
Abduljabbar, Maram H.
Alzhrani, Rami M.
Alnemari, Reem M.
Aldhafeeri, Muneef M.
Serag, Ahmed
Almalki, Atiah H.
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel analytical method was developed for the determination of bupropion in pharmaceutical formulations and spiked plasma samples using N, S co-doped carbon quantum dots (N,S CQDs) as a fluorescent probe. The N,S CQDs were thoroughly characterized and its optical properties were investigated. The developed N,S CQDs exhibited blue emission at 435 nm upon excitation at 357 nm. Detailed studies on the sensing mechanism revealed that the fluorescence quenching of the N,S CQDs by bupropion follows a static quenching mechanism, as confirmed by Stern-Volmer analysis and thermodynamic parameters. The quenching parameters were further optimized using a central composite experimental design, and the method was validated according to ICH guidelines. The method displayed excellent linearity in the range of 0.05–1.5 μg/mL, with a limit of detection of 0.016 μg/mL. The developed method was successfully applied for the analysis of bupropion in pharmaceutical formulations and spiked human plasma samples, with satisfactory recoveries. Moreover, statistical analysis revealed no significant differences between the results obtained using the proposed method and a reference HPLC method, indicating the reliability of the developed approach. Greenness and blueness of the method was evaluated using the AGREE and BAGI tools respectively, demonstrating the environmentally friendly and analytical practicality of the proposed technique.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bupropion
N,S CQDs
Fluorescence quenching
Greenness assessment

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization of the fluorescence sensing of bupropion
Determination of bupropion in pharmaceutical formulations, spiked plasma and environmental samples
Characterization and optical properties of N,S CQDs
Sensing mechanism of N,S CQDs towards bupropion
Optimized conditions for the fluorescence quenching method
Determination of bupropion in pharmaceutical formulations, spiked plasma and environmental samples
Comparative evaluation of the greenness and blueness of the proposed method

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors extend their appreciation to Taif University, Saudi Arabia, for supporting this work through project number (TU-DSPP-2024-82).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization and spectral characteristics of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (N,S CQDs). (A) Dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis showing a particle size distribution with an average size of 5.13 nm and a standard deviation of 1.7 nm. (B) High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image displaying spherical and monodisperse particles with a scale bar of 10 nm. (C) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of N,S CQDs highlighting a characteristic peak at 337 nm; the inset shows the aqueous dispersion under UV light, emitting bright blue fluorescence. (D) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of N,S CQDs, with excitation centered at 357 nm and emission peaking at approximately 435 nm.

Fluorescence quenching and thermodynamic analysis of the interaction between N, S CQDs and bupropion. (A) Fluorescence spectra of N, S CQDs at increasing concentrations of bupropion (0 to 1.5 µg/mL), showing a concentration-dependent decrease in fluorescence intensity. (B) Stern-Volmer plots (F0/F vs. concentration) at different temperatures (298 K, 303 K, and 313 K), indicating a static quenching mechanism. (C) Modified Stern-Volmer plots (F0/(F0−F) vs. 1/[Q]) used to determine association constant (Ka) between N, S CQDs and bupropion. (D) van't Hoff plot (lnKa vs. 1/T), illustrating the thermodynamic feasibility of the interaction and revealing binding forces involved.

Visualization of individual factors affecting the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F) between N,S CQDs and bupropion, analyzed using a central composite design. (A) Effect of pH, showing non-linearity and an optimal response near slightly alkaline conditions. (B) Influence of buffer volume (0.5–2.0 mL), exhibiting a predominantly linear trend. (C) Effect of N,S CQDs volume (0.5–2.0 mL), displaying a subtle non-linear relationship. (D) Influence of incubation time (2–10 min), with minimal variation in quenching efficiency.

Interaction between pH and N,S CQDs volume on fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F), visualized through (A) a 2D interaction plot, showing non-linear trends with optimal quenching observed at slightly alkaline pH (7.5–10.0) and higher N,S CQDs volumes (1.5–2.0 mL), and (B) a 3D surface response plot, highlighting the combined effects of both factors on the fluorescence quenching response.

Evaluation of selectivity and interference showing a significantly higher fluorescence quenching ratio for bupropion compared to pharmaceutical excipients, common ions, biological components and pooled plasma, confirming the method's high selectivity.

Comparative evaluation of greenness and blueness between the developed fluorescence quenching method and the reported HPLC method. (A, B) AGREE metric scores for the fluorescence quenching method (0.77) and the HPLC method (0.66), demonstrating the superior greenness of the developed method. (C, D) BAGI index scores for the fluorescence quenching method (75) and the HPLC method (77.5), showing comparable practicality and ease-of-use for both techniques.

ANOVA results showing significant effects of pH (A), buffer volume (B), N,S CQD concentration (C), and interaction terms on the fluorescence quenching method (p < 0.05), with non-significant lack of fit (p = 0.2043).
Validation parameters of the developed fluorescence quenching method for bupropion analysis.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Average of 9 determinations (3 concentrations repeated 3 times).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: % RSD of 9 determinations (3 concentrations repeated 3 times) measured on the same day.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: % RSD of 9 determinations (3 concentrations repeated 3 times) measured in the three consecutive days.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Statistical evaluation and equivalence testing of the developed fluorescence quenching method and the reported HPLC method [14] for bupropion determination in pharmaceutical dosage form.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The values in parenthesis are tabulated values of “t “and “F” at (P = 0.05).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recovery studies for bupropion determination in spiked plasma and environmental water samples (river and tap water) using the N,S CQD fluorescence quenching method.

head.ce:title: Spectrofluorimetric determination of bupropion using N,S co-doped carbon quantum dots: Mechanistic investigation, response surface optimization, and application to pharmaceutical formulations, spiked plasma and environmental samples

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a novel analytical method was developed for the determination of bupropion in pharmaceutical formulations and spiked plasma samples using N, S co-doped carbon quantum dots (N,S CQDs) as a fluorescent probe. The N,S CQDs were thoroughly characterized and its optical properties were investigated. The developed N,S CQDs exhibited blue emission at 435 nm upon excitation at 357 nm. Detailed studies on the sensing mechanism revealed that the fluorescence quenching of the N,S CQDs by bupropion follows a static quenching mechanism, as confirmed by Stern-Volmer analysis and thermodynamic parameters. The quenching parameters were further optimized using a central composite experimental design, and the method was validated according to ICH guidelines. The method displayed excellent linearity in the range of 0.05–1.5 μg/mL, with a limit of detection of 0.016 μg/mL. The developed method was successfully applied for the analysis of bupropion in pharmaceutical formulations and spiked human plasma samples, with satisfactory recoveries. Moreover, statistical analysis revealed no significant differences between the results obtained using the proposed method and a reference HPLC method, indicating the reliability of the developed approach. Greenness and blueness of the method was evaluated using the AGREE and BAGI tools respectively, demonstrating the environmentally friendly and analytical practicality of the proposed technique.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent nanomaterials have been extensively studied in recent years due to their unique optical properties, high sensitivity, and selectivity, making them attractive for various analytical applications, including the determination of pharmaceuticals in dosage forms and biological samples [1,2]. Among these nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have garnered significant attention due to their exceptional characteristics, such as tunable emission, high photostability, and facile synthesis as well as their potential for eco-friendly and cost-effective analytical methods [3–5]. However, one of the major challenges in the development of CQDs-based sensors is their low quantum yield, which can be improved through doping with heteroatoms, such as nitrogen and sulfur [6]. Such approach not only enhances the fluorescence properties of the CQDs but also introduces additional binding sites for target analytes, leading to improved sensitivity and selectivity [7,8]. Furthermore, the similar atomic radii and electronegativity of nitrogen and sulfur compared to carbon can facilitate the incorporation of these heteroatoms into the carbon lattice, resulting in enhanced optical and electronic properties [9]. Recently, N,S co-doped carbon quantum dots (N,S CQDs) have been successfully employed for the determination of various analytes, including some pharmaceutically-important nitro compounds such as rifampicin, tinidazole, ornidazole, and metronidazole through quenching of the native fluorescence of these nanomaterial [10]. Interestingly, this approach holds great promise for the development of sensitive and selective analytical methods for the determination of other pharmaceuticals.

Bupropion is a widely-prescribed antidepressant drug that is also used for smoking cessation [11,12]. Its mechanism of action involves the inhibition of norepinephrine and dopamine reuptake thus elevating the levels of these neurotransmitters in the brain [13]. Due to its widespread use and potential for abuse, accurate and reliable analytical methods are required for the determination of bupropion in pharmaceutical formulations and biological samples. Literature review reveals that various analytical techniques, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [14–16], liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) [17,18], and electrochemical methods [19], have been reported for the determination of bupropion. However, these techniques often require complex sample preparation, expensive instrumentation, and experienced personnel, which may not be readily available in all laboratories. Spectrophotometric methods have also been reported, but they generally suffer from poor sensitivity and selectivity [14,19]. On the other hand, no spectrofluorimetric method utilizing N,S CQDs for the determination of bupropion has been reported to date. Spectrofluorimetry is an attractive analytical technique due to its high sensitivity, selectivity, and ease of implementation, making it a suitable alternative to the existing methods as well as its environmental friendliness posing as a “green” analytical approach [20].

Although the application of N,S CQDs for the determination of pharmaceuticals seems forward, the underlying sensing mechanism and factors affecting the quenching process are need to be investigated in detail to ensure the reliability and robustness of the proposed analytical method. In this context, design of experiments based on the central composite or Box-Behnken design can be a powerful tool for the optimization of the critical parameters affecting the quenching process, such as pH, reaction time, and CQDs concentration [21,22]. Furthermore, the analytical validation of the developed method according to the ICH guidelines is essential to demonstrate its suitability for the intended application.

The present study aims to develop a sensitive and selective spectrofluorimetric method for the determination of bupropion using N,S CQDs as a fluorescent probe. Characterization of the synthesized N,S CQDs will be carried out to investigate their size, morphology, and optical properties. The sensing mechanism and thermodynamics of the bupropion-N,S CQDs interaction will be elucidated using Stern-Volmer analysis as well as thermodynamics calculations. The critical parameters affecting the quenching process will be optimized using a central composite design approach. The developed method will be validated according to the ICH guidelines and applied for the determination of bupropion in pharmaceutical formulations and spiked human plasma samples. Finally, the greenness and blueness of the proposed method will be assessed to demonstrate its environmental benignity and analytical practicality compared to the standard chromatographic procedure.

Bupropion hydrochloride with purity of 99.8 % was obtained from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Citric acid, thiourea and sodium hydroxide as well as other analytical grade reagents were purchased from PioChem Co. (Cairo, Egypt). HPLC grade acetone and acetonitrile were procured from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). The pharmaceutical formulation containing bupropion hydrochloride (Wellinta® SR 150 mg) was obtained from a local pharmacy, Cairo, Egypt. Distilled water was used throughout the study. Britton-Robinson buffer solutions at different pH ranges were prepared by mixing appropriate volumes of 0.04 M acetic acid, 0.04 M phosphoric acid, and 0.04 M boric acid, and adjusting the pH using 0.2 M sodium hydroxide.

The fluorescence measurements were carried out using a Jasco FP-6200 spectrofluorometer equipped with a 150 W xenon lamp as the excitation source and 1 cm quartz cells. The acquisition parameters were set at the following: excitation and emission slit widths 10 nm, scan speed 200 nm/min, and medium sensitivity. UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-1800 spectrophotometer using 1 cm quartz cells. pH measurements were performed using a Jenway 3510 pH meter. The size and morphology of the N,S CQDs were analyzed using a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope operated at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The hydrodynamic diameter and of the N,S CQDs were determined using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS instrument.

The N,S CQDs s were synthesized by a one-pot hydrothermal method using citric acid and thiourea as the precursors. Briefly, a solution containing citric acid (1 g) and thiourea (0.5 g) was prepared in 20 mL of distilled water. The solution was then transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 6 h. After the reaction, the resulting solution was allowed to cool to room temperature and then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove any large particles. The supernatant containing the N,S CQDs was collected then precipitated with acetone, centrifuged, and collected for further characterization and applications. A stock solution of the N,S CQDs was prepared in distilled water at a concentration of 1 mg/mL and stored at 4 °C in the dark for further use. The synthesized quantum dots were characterized using various techniques to investigate their size, morphology, and optical properties. The size and morphology were analyzed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and dynamic light scattering (DLS). The optical properties, including UV–Vis absorption and emission spectra, were studied to understand the fluorescence behavior of the N,S CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the fluorescence sensing of bupropion
ce:section.ce:para: The factors influencing the fluorescence quenching of the N,S CQDs by bupropion, such as pH, buffer volume, volume of N,S CQDs and reaction time, were optimized using a central composite design approach. Initial screening studies were carried out to determine the suitable ranges of the critical parameters. The studied pH range was 4–10, the volume of Britton-Robinson buffer was 0.5–2.0 mL, the volume of CQD solution was 0.5–2.0 mL, and the reaction time was 2–10 min. Axial, central and factorial points were included in the experimental design to fit a quadratic model resulting in 29 experimental runs. The fluorescence intensity of the N,S CQDs was measured with (F) and without bupropion (F0) to calculate the fluorescence quenching. The excitation and emission wavelengths were set at 357 and 435 nm, respectively, based on the previous characterization studies. The ratio of the fluorescence intensities (F0/F) was employed as the response in the data analysis. Then, ANOVA was performed to evaluate the statistical significance of the model and the individual factors using Design-Expert software. Numerical optimization was carried out to determine the optimum values of the critical parameters to achieve maximum fluorescence quenching for bupropion detection. The optimum conditions employed for further studies as revealed by the design include pH 9.1, 2.0 mL of Britton-Robinson buffer, and 2.0 mL of N,S CQDs solution with a reaction time of 3 min that have been used for further validation and applications.

The developed fluorescence quenching method for the detection of bupropion using N,S CQDs was validated as per the ICH guidelines. A set of parameters including linearity, limits of detection and quantitation, precision, accuracy, and selectivity were investigated. To a series of 10 mL volumetric flasks, different volumes of a standard solution of bupropion were added to achieve the final concentrations in the range of 0.05–1.5 μg/mL. Then, 2.0 mL of Britton-Robinson buffer (pH 9.1) was added followed by 2.0 mL of the N,S CQDs solution. The mixture was allowed to stand for 3 min before being diluted to the final volume with distilled water. The fluorescence intensity was measured at 435 nm upon excitation at 357 nm, and the ratio of the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of bupropion was calculated. Linearity was established by plotting the fluorescence quenching ratio (F0/F) as a function of bupropion concentration in the range of 0.05–1.5 μg/mL. The regression equation was determined, and the coefficient of determination (r2) was calculated to evaluate the linearity. The limits of detection and quantitation were calculated based on the standard deviation of the blank and the slope of the calibration curve.

Accuracy was evaluated by analyzing the recovery of bupropion at three different concentration levels in triplicate (0.1, 0.5, and 1.0 μg/mL). Precision was assessed in terms of repeatability (intra-day) and intermediate precision (inter-day) by analyzing the standard solutions at three different concentrations 0.1, 0.5, and 1.0 μg/mL in triplicate on the same day and on 3 different days, respectively. The selectivity of the method was investigated by studying the possible interference from common pharmaceutical excipients that might be present in the dosage forms as well as other ions such as Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+, K+, SO4 2− and PO4 3 at concentrations 10-fold higher than the analyte. Furthermore, standard addition experiments were performed to evaluate the matrix effect in the analysis of pharmaceutical samples where known amounts of bupropion were added to the sample solutions and the recovery was calculated. Robustness was examined by making small, deliberate changes in the optimized experimental conditions, such as pH, volume of buffer, and volume of N,S CQDs solution, and observing the effect on the fluorescence quenching response.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of bupropion in pharmaceutical formulations, spiked plasma and environmental samples
ce:section.ce:para: The validated fluorescence quenching method was applied to determine the bupropion content in commercial pharmaceutical formulations and spiked human plasma samples. For the pharmaceutical formulation, 5 tablets were accurately weighed, finely powdered, and an amount equivalent to 10 mg of bupropion was dissolved in 80 mL of distilled water. The solution was sonicated for 10 min, completed to 100 mL with distilled water, and filtered. Aliquots of the filtrate were transferred to 10 mL volumetric flasks, and the developed fluorescence method was applied.

For plasma analysis, 1 mL of blank human plasma was spiked with bupropion standard solutions to achieve initial concentrations of 1.0, 2.5, 5.0, and 10.0 μg/mL, corresponding to final concentrations of 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, and 1.0 μg/mL after dilution. The spiked plasma samples were pretreated by protein precipitation using 3 mL acetonitrile, followed by vortexing for 2 min and centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was collected and transferred to a 10 mL volumetric flask, then diluted to volume with distilled water to ensure analyte concentrations fell within the method's linear range (0.05–1.5 μg/mL).

Environmental water samples (river and tap water) were filtered through a 0.45 μm membrane filter to remove suspended particles. Aliquots of 1 mL filtered water were spiked with bupropion to achieve the same concentration levels as plasma samples (0.1–1.0 μg/mL final concentrations). The spiked water samples underwent salting-out assisted liquid–liquid extraction by adding 3 mL acetonitrile and 250 mg NaCl to facilitate phase separation. The mixture was vortexed for 2 min and centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min. The upper acetonitrile layer was collected, transferred to a 10 mL volumetric flask, and diluted to volume with distilled water. All sample analyses were performed in triplicate under optimized fluorescence measurement conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and optical properties of N,S CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized N,S CQDs were characterized using various techniques including DLS, TEM, and UV–Vis and fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. 1 ). The average hydrodynamic diameter of the N,S CQDs determined by DLS was 5.13 ± 1.7 nm, indicating the formation of small-sized particles (Fig. 1A). TEM imaging provided further insight into the morphology and size of the particles, showing that the N,S CQDs are quasi-spherical with an average size of 5.09 ± 1.97 nm (Fig. 1B). This range aligns well with the DLS data, confirming the consistency of the size measurements. The optical properties of the N,S CQDs were evaluated using UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. The UV–Vis spectrum exhibited a characteristic absorption peak at approximately 337 nm (Fig. 1C), which corresponds to n–π* transitions associated with CO and CN functional groups on the CQD surface. This peak is indicative of surface passivation and doping effects introduced by nitrogen and sulfur atoms. Additionally, the solution under UV light (365 nm) excitation emitted bright blue fluorescence, further emphasizing the luminescent nature of the N,S CQDs (Fig. 1C, inset). Fluorescence spectroscopy analysis showed a prominent excitation peak at 357 nm and a corresponding emission peak at 435 nm, as illustrated in (Fig. 1D). The significant Stoke’s shift observed between the excitation and emission maxima suggests the presence of surface states and quantum confinement effects by the functional groups on the CQD surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism of N,S CQDs towards bupropion
ce:section.ce:para:
The sensing mechanism of the N,S CQDs towards bupropion was investigated using Stern-Volmer analysis and thermodynamic studies (Fig. 2 ). The fluorescence quenching of the N,S CQDs by bupropion was evaluated by measuring the fluorescence intensities in the absence (F0) and presence (F) of the analyte at different concentrations. A concentration dependent quenching of the N,S CQD fluorescence was observed, indicating the efficient interaction between the CQDs and bupropion (Fig. 2A). The Stern-Volmer plot showed a linear relationship between the fluorescence quenching ratio (F0/F) and bupropion concentration which decreased as the temperature increased from 298 to 318 K, suggesting a static quenching mechanism (Fig. 2B). The Stern-Volmer quenching constant was calculated from the slope of the Stern-Volmer plot:
F 0
F
= 1
+
K
s v
[ Q
where Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant and and [Q] is the molar concentration of bupropion. The Ksv values were determined to be 1.23 × 105, 1.08 × 105, and 0.97 × 105 M−1 at 298, 308, and 318 K, respectively, indicating a decrease in the quenching efficiency at higher temperatures typical for static quenching mechanism. Additionally, from the Ksv values, the quenching rate constant (kq) was calculated using the equation:
K
s v
=
K
q
τ 0
where τ0 is the average lifetime of the N,S CQDs in the absence of the quencher, which was determined to be 10−8 s. The calculated Kq values were in the order of 1013 L/mol/s, which is much higher than the diffusion-controlled limit of 1010 L/mol/s, further confirming the static quenching mechanism.

The modified Stern-Volmer plot (Fig. 2C) was also employed to determine the association constant:
F 0
F 0 - F
=
1
[ Q ] K a
+
1
K a
where Ka is the association constant. The obtained Ka values were 2.65 × 105, 2.07 × 105, and 1.34 × 105 M−1 at 298, 308, and 318 K, respectively, further confirming the static quenching mechanism.

The thermodynamic parameters, such as Gibbs free energy (ΔG°), enthalpy (ΔH°), and entropy (ΔS°), were calculated using the Van't Hoff equation (Fig. 2D). The negative values of ΔG indicate the spontaneous nature of the interaction between the N,S CQDs and bupropion, which increased as the temperature increased. Negative ΔH and ΔS values suggest that the interaction is enthalpy-driven and accompanied by a decrease in randomness of the system which is consistent with a static quenching mechanism where the analyte formed a non-fluorescent complex with the N,S CQDs leading to a decrease in the system entropy. The detailed thermodynamic parameters are provided in (Table S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimized conditions for the fluorescence quenching method
ce:section.ce:para:
The factors affecting the fluorescence quenching of the N,S CQDs by bupropion were optimized using a central composite design. The four variables investigated were pH, buffer volume, N,S CQD concentration and incubation time. Axial points at alpha = 1.2 were employed to ensure a rotatable design. The quenching ratio F0/F was chosen as the response variable. The ratios ranged from 1.05 to 1.62 under the different experimental conditions. Since the min max ratio is quite low, no transformation was needed. A quadratic model was derived from the experimental data with good fit (R2 = 0.956) and insignificant lack of fit. However, backward elimination of non-significant parameters resulted in a simplified model equation:
R = 1.43014 + 0.111816 A + 0.0420983 B + 0.0815947 C + 0.0707138 A C + - 0.127296
A 2
+ - 0.0426431
C 2
where R is the quenching ratio, A, B, C are the pH, buffer volume, and N,S CQD concentration, respectively. These results are consistent when investigating the individual effects of each variable (Fig. 3 ). Clearly, a quadratic effect of pH and N,S CQD concentration on the quenching was observed, while the buffer volume only had a linear effect. The time did not have a significant influence on the quenching ratio within the studied range. The positive coefficient for A, B, and C indicates a favorable effect on the quenching, while the negative coefficient for A2 and C2 suggests an optimal value and a decreasing trend beyond that point. The observed effects on the fluorescence quenching could be attributed to the influence of protonation and deprotonation equilibria on the surface functional groups of the N,S CQD. At lower pH values, excessive protonation may hinder the interaction between the N,S CQD and bupropion, reducing the quenching efficiency. Conversely, at higher pH levels, deprotonation of surface groups enhances electrostatic and hydrogen bonding interactions, improving quenching. However, beyond a certain pH (around 9), the destabilization of the N,S CQD or changes in the electronic states of bupropion could reduce quenching efficiency, leading to the observed decline. A similar trend was observed with increasing N,S CQD concentration, where excessive N,S CQD could result in self-quenching or aggregation, limiting the interaction with the analyte.

ANOVA analysis of the reduced model showed that it was highly significant (p < 0.0001) with a high coefficient of determination (R2 = 0.930) (Table 1 ). A close agreement between the adjusted (0.912) and predicted (0.865) R2 values indicated the good predictive ability of the model. The CV% was less than 4 % confirming the reliability of the method. Besides, adequate precision was 20.858, indicating a good signal-to-noise ratio. Examining the diagnostic plots revealed that the model assumptions are valid with the data points randomly scattered around the diagonal line in the normal probability plot, confirming the normal distribution of residuals (Fig. S1A). Moreover, the actual versus predicted plot showed a linear trend, indicating the good predictive ability of the model (Fig. S1B). The influence of run order on the response was insignificant as evident from the random scattering in the residual versus run order plot (Fig. S1A). Besides, the leverage versus run order plot suggested no unusual data points (Fig. S2B). These comprehensive statistical analyses demonstrate the validity of the reduced quadratic model for modelling the fluorescence quenching of N,S CQDs by bupropion.

Another advantage of the design of experiments approach is that it allows studying the interaction effects among the variables through 2D-interaction and 3D-surface plots. Since the only significant interaction was between pH and N,S CQD concentration, the corresponding 2D interaction and 3D surface plots are shown in (Fig. 4 ). The plots clearly indicate that the quenching ratio increases as both the pH and N,S CQD concentration increase, which is consistent with the positive coefficients for these terms in the model equation. However, the quenching ratio decreases beyond certain values of pH and N,S CQD concentration, as indicated by the negative coefficients for the quadratic terms. Numerical optimization using the desirability function approach yielded the following optimum conditions: pH 9.1, buffer volume 2.0 mL, N,S CQD concentration 200 μg/mL and incubation time 3 min which resulted in the maximum quenching ratio (Fig. S3).

The developed fluorescence quenching method was validated according to ICH guidelines (Table 2 ). Linearity was evaluated by analyzing 7 standard solutions of bupropion in the range of 0.05–1.5 μg/mL. Regression analysis showed an excellent linear fit with a coefficient of determination (r2) of 0.9997 and a linear regression equation of:
F 0 / F = 1.1008 + 0.5131
C
where C is the bupropion concentration in μg/mL. The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantitation (LOQ) were calculated to be 0.016 μg/mL and 0.048 μg/mL, respectively, indicating the high sensitivity of the proposed method (Table 2). The accuracy of the method was determined by analyzing three concentration levels of bupropion (0.1, 0.5, and 1 μg/mL) in triplicate. The mean percent recovery was 99.69 ± 0.885 %, which confirms the accuracy of the method. The method precision was evaluated by determining the intraday and interday precision which gave RSD values less than 1.0 % and 1.5 %, respectively, demonstrating the high precision of the method (Table 2).

The selectivity of the developed method was evaluated by analyzing placebo samples containing common pharmaceutical excipients such as lactose, starch, magnesium stearate, and cellulose. The results showed no significant interference, indicating the high selectivity of the method (Fig. 5 ). Furthermore, standard addition experiments conducted in the presence of the formulation excipients yielded good recoveries of 98.79–100.77 %, demonstrating the accuracy and reliability of the method for the quantitation of bupropion in the formulation (Table S3). Additionally, other interfering ions like Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+, K+, SO4 2− and PO4 3− did not show any significant effect on the quenching ratio within the tested concentrations (Fig. 5). Importantly, pooled plasma samples and biological components also did not exhibit any interference, confirming the suitability of the method for the determination of bupropion in spiked plasma samples (Fig. 5). Robustness of the method was evaluated by introducing small deliberate changes in pH ± 0.2, buffer volume ± 0.2 mL and N,S CQD concentration ± 10 μg/mL. The method was found to be robust as the changes did not significantly affect the quenching ratio, with RSD values less than 2 % (Table 2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of bupropion in pharmaceutical formulations, spiked plasma and environmental samples
ce:section.ce:para: The validated fluorescence quenching method was applied for the determination of bupropion in tablet formulations and spiked human plasma samples. Commercial bupropion tablets were analyzed, and the bupropion content was found to be 99.84 ± 1.011 % of the labeled claim (Table 3 ). Statistical comparison with the reported HPLC method [14] showed no significant difference at a 95 % confidence level, in terms of mean bupropion content as indicated by the calculated t-value (0.598) being less than the critical t-value (2.306). F test also revealed that the variances of the two methods did not differ significantly where the calculated F value was less than the critical F value (Table 3). Interval hypothesis testing further confirmed the equivalence of the two methods with lower and upper bounds of the confidence interval for the difference in means being within the ± 2 % acceptance range (Table 3).

For the analysis of spiked plasma samples, bupropion was extracted using a simple liquid–liquid extraction procedure with acetonitrile. The recovery of bupropion in spiked plasma samples ranged from 95.72 % to 102.70 %, with RSD values between 1.298 % and 3.888 %, demonstrating the reliability of the method for biological matrices (Table 4 ). The method was further extended to environmental water analysis, where satisfactory results were obtained for both river and tap water samples. The analysis of spiked river water samples showed recovery rates between 95.55 % and 101.70 % with RSD values ≤ 3.288 %, while tap water analysis yielded recoveries ranging from 95.99 % to 104.82 % with RSD values ≤ 3.138 %. These results indicate the broad applicability of the developed method for determining bupropion in various environmental water sources with high accuracy and precision (Table 4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparative evaluation of the greenness and blueness of the proposed method
ce:section.ce:para: An important aspect of the present study is the evaluation of the greenness and blueness of the developed fluorescence quenching method in comparison with the reported HPLC method [14] for the determination of bupropion. An initial evaluation revealed that the proposed method is more benign than the reported HPLC method in terms of safety to humans and the environment. Water and aqueous buffer system were used as the only solvents, whereas the HPLC method employed toxic solvents like acetonitrile. The energy required for the fluorescence measurement is significantly lower than that for HPLC. Additionally, the waste generated from the fluorescence-based method is lower, all of which contributes to the enhanced greenness of the proposed method. In order to quantify the greenness, the AGREE metric was employed which is a comprehensive tool for the evaluation of the overall greenness of an analytical method based on 12 principals of green analytical chemistry [23]. The AGREE score calculated for the proposed fluorescence quenching method was 0.77 (Fig. 6 A) compared to 0.66 for the HPLC method (Fig. 6B), confirming the superior greenness of the developed method. The most significant contributors to the greenness of the fluorescence quenching method are factors 7–10 related to waste generation, the throughput of the analysis, energy requirements and types of reagents and solvents used, respectively. The developed method transcended the chromatographic approach in these terms by a considerable margin thus establishing the greenness advantage.

In order to assess the analytical practicality of the developed method in terms of simplicity and ease-of-use, the blueness of the method was evaluated using the BAGI index which consider factors such as the availability of the instrumentation, and other practical considerations such as sample preparation, and operational requirements [24]. The BAGI score for the fluorescence quenching method was 75 (Fig. 6C) compared to 77.5 for the HPLC method (Fig. 6D), suggesting comparable blueness between the two techniques. The lower blueness score of the developed method is mainly attributed to the degree of automation which is relatively higher for the HPLC method compared to the manual fluorescence measurements. However, the simplicity of the sample preparation, and the availability of the fluorescence spectrophotometer which is a commonly available instrument in most analytical laboratories compensates for this disadvantage to some extent. Overall, the developed fluorescence quenching method exhibited superior greenness compared to the HPLC method, with comparable blueness, highlighting its potential as a sustainable and practical alternative for the determination of bupropion in pharmaceutical formulations and biological samples.

The present study successfully demonstrated the development and validation of a sensitive, selective, and green fluorescence quenching-based method for the determination of bupropion using N,S co-doped CQDs as a fluorescent probe. Stern Volmer analysis and thermodynamic studies provided valuable insights into the interaction and quenching mechanism. The factors that affect the quenching process were optimized using central composite design, ensuring robust and reliable quantification. The developed method was validated as per ICH guidelines and demonstrated good linearity in the range of 0.05–1.5 μg/mL, with limits of detection and quantification as low as 0.016 and 0.048 μg/mL, respectively. Application to real pharmaceutical formulations confirmed the reliability and green credentials of the method in comparison to the reported HPLC approach as indicated by statistical tests. Besides, the method was successfully applied for the determination of bupropion in spiked plasma samples with high recoveries, demonstrating its potential for bioanalytical applications.

A major advantage of the proposed fluorescence quenching method is its superior greenness compared to the conventional HPLC approach, as indicated by the higher AGREE score 0.77 vs 0.66. Future work could focus on exploring the utility of the N,S CQDs as multifunctional platforms for simultaneous detection and quantification of other psychoactive drugs in a single assay, thereby expanding the scope and applicability of the proposed green analytical methodology. Additionally, further simplification of the sample preparation steps and automation of the fluorescence measurements could enhance the blueness and overall practicality of the method for routine pharmaceutical and bioanalytical applications.
This research was funded by Taif University, Saudi Arabia, Project No. (TU-DSPP-2024-82).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors extend their appreciation to Taif University, Saudi Arabia, for supporting this work through project number (TU-DSPP-2024-82).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2025.126000.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
